questions.scenario: The team wants to delete all temporary resources from a load test next week without touching production. They do not need a new billing boundary. Which Azure container is the best fit for that cleanup shape?

options.text: A separate resource group for the load test resources, with clear tags and an owner.
A separate subscription, because the load test has a different budget owner even if the access model stays simple.
A separate availability zone, because zones group resources by lifecycle.
A separate public DNS record, because DNS records delete all resources behind them.

questions.explanation: Resource groups are useful lifecycle containers. If the goal is cleanup without a separate billing or policy boundary, a dedicated resource group is often enough.

questions.scenario: A production API, production database, and production Key Vault are all part of the same release lifecycle. A teammate proposes placing each in a different resource group because they are different service types. What is the better first question?

options.text: Do these resources change, review, and retire together, or do they need separate owners and lifecycles?
Which resources are most sensitive, because sensitive resources may need separate groups even when releases happen together.
Which service was created first, because first-created resources own the resource group.
Which resource is cheapest, because cost decides whether resources can share a group.

questions.explanation: Resource group design should follow lifecycle and ownership, not service category alone. Different service types can belong together when they are operated together.

questions.scenario: A new teammate keeps using `subscription` and `resource group` as if they mean the same thing. The team is reviewing production boundaries, cleanup behavior, and billing ownership before creating the next environment. Which statements separate subscriptions from resource groups correctly?

options.text: Subscriptions are commonly used for billing, quota, policy, and broad access boundaries.
Resource groups are commonly used to keep related resources together for lifecycle, ownership, and deployment organization.
Deleting a resource group can delete the resources inside it, so the lifecycle choice matters.
A resource group can contain resources from any subscription if their names match.

questions.explanation: Subscriptions and resource groups are different scopes. Resource groups live inside a subscription, and deleting a group can remove its resources. Name similarity does not let a resource group span subscriptions.

questions.scenario:
An audit shows this shape:

- Subscription: `sub-devpolaris-prod`
- Resource group: `rg-orders-api`
- Resource group: `rg-orders-database`
- Resource group: `rg-orders-temp-migration-2026`

The migration group is no longer used. What should the team verify before deleting it?

options.text: Whether every resource in that resource group is truly migration-only, using resource IDs, tags, dependencies, and owners.
Whether the group name contains `temp`, because the name alone is proof that deletion is safe.
Whether the subscription has another resource group, because Azure requires one group to remain after deletion.
Whether any resources in other regions depend on resources in the migration group.

questions.explanation: Resource group deletion can be powerful and risky. The team should verify actual contents and dependencies, not trust the name alone.

questions.scenario: A team creates `rg-devpolaris-orders-prod` in `sub-devpolaris-dev` by accident. The name says prod, but the subscription says dev. Which evidence should automation trust for scope?

options.text: The full resource ID and subscription context, then tags and owner review as supporting evidence.
The resource group name and tags first, because they describe the environment humans intended.
The region and resource type first, because those usually stay stable across environments.
The service icon in the portal, because icons show environment boundaries.

questions.explanation: A full resource ID shows the subscription and resource group path. Names and tags help humans, but they do not override scope.
